claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 14 years old and does not live with you.
  - Blake is 19 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 14 (under 16)
  - Someone who lives with the child is already claiming
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 19 and in approved education
  - Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Someone who lives with the child is already claiming
circumstances: Someone who lives with the child is already claiming
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
circumstances: Child is 19 and in approved education
Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's begin the Child Benefit eligibility assessment.

**First, I need to know about your child's age:**

How old is the child you're claiming Child Benefit for?

- **Under 16 years old**
- **16 to 19 years old**
- **20 years old or over**

Please let me know which age category applies to your child. If you have multiple children, we can go through each one - just let me know the age of the first child you'd like to check.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi, thanks for your help. I'm interested in claiming Child Benefit and want to know if I'm eligible. I have two children - Alex who is 14, and Blake who is 19.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! Thanks for reaching out. I'd be happy to help you determine your Child Benefit eligibility for both Alex (14) and Blake (19).

Let's go through each child one at a time.

---

**Starting with Alex (age 14):**

Since Alex is under 16, they automatically qualify based on age. Let me check the next requirement.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Question for Alex (age 14):**

Is Alex working **less than 24 hours per week** (or not in paid work at all)?

This is usually a straightforward "yes" for most 14-year-olds, but I need to confirm before we proceed.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't work at all - they're only 14, still in school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's what I expected for a 14-year-old. Let me continue checking the requirements for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question for Alex:**

Is Alex **NOT receiving any benefits in their own right**, such as Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit?

(In other words, Alex doesn't claim these benefits themselves?)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right, Alex doesn't get any benefits in their own name.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question for Alex:**

Is Alex currently in an apprenticeship?

- If **no** (not in any apprenticeship), that's fine
- If **yes**, I'll need to know where and what type, as some apprenticeships disqualify a child from Child Benefit (e.g., most apprenticeships in England and Scotland), while others are approved (e.g., Foundation Apprenticeships in Wales)

Since Alex is 14 and still in school, I'm guessing they're not in an apprenticeship - is that correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's correct, Alex isn't doing an apprenticeship - they're just in regular school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to check your residency status. This applies to you as the claimant (and will also apply for Blake, so we only need to confirm this once).

**Do you meet any of the following UK residency requirements?**

You're eligible if **ANY ONE** of these applies to you:

1. **You normally live in the UK**
2. **You have settled status** under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. **You have pre-settled status** AND are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold for 3+ continuous months)
4. **You have pre-settled status** AND are actively seeking work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
5. **You have pre-settled status** AND have sufficient resources to support yourself (without claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit)
6. **You have pre-settled status** AND are a student with sufficient resources
7. **You have pre-settled status** AND are a family member of an EEA/Swiss national with right to reside
8. **You're a Crown servant** living abroad (or in certain qualifying countries)

Do any of these apply to you? For most people, option 1 (normally living in the UK) is the most common.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, living in the UK in Manchester satisfies the residency requirement.

**Next question about Alex:**

Are you responsible for Alex? You're considered responsible if **ANY ONE** of these applies:

1. **You live with Alex**
2. **You contribute to Alex's upkeep** – at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent) through things like food, clothes, pocket money, housing, or presents
3. **You pay maintenance** for Alex under a court order or agreement

Which of these applies to your situation with Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex doesn't live with me - they live with my ex-partner.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I understand. Since Alex doesn't live with you, let me ask a follow-up question:

**Do you contribute to Alex's upkeep?**

This could include:
- Paying for food, clothes, pocket money, housing, or presents
- Making maintenance payments under a court order or agreement

To qualify, your contributions need to be at least equal to the amount of Child Benefit (currently around £25.60 per week for the eldest child, or £16.95 for additional children).

Do you contribute financially to Alex's care in any of these ways?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I'm not currently contributing financially to Alex's care.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for being upfront about that. Unfortunately, this means you wouldn't be eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I'm sorry, but **for Alex, you are not eligible** for Child Benefit because you need to be responsible for the child. This means either:
- Living with them, OR
- Contributing at least the Child Benefit amount towards their care

Since Alex lives with your ex-partner and you're not currently contributing financially, your ex-partner may be the one eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex instead.

---

**Now let's check your eligibility for Blake (age 19):**

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Since Blake is 19, there are additional requirements for Child Benefit eligibility. For 16-19 year olds, they need to be in approved education or training.

**Question about Blake:**

Which of the following applies to Blake?

1. **Currently in education or training** (e.g., A-levels, college, approved training programme)
2. **Recently left education** (within the last 20 weeks)
3. **Neither** – not in education/training and hasn't recently left

Which situation best describes Blake?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Blake is currently in education - studying for A-levels.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
A-levels are an approved qualification. I just need to confirm a couple more details about Blake's education:

**Is Blake studying full-time?**

To qualify, the course must involve at least **12 hours per week of supervised study**. 

Also, is the course:
- **NOT funded by an employer**, and
- **NOT part of an agreement for a future job with an employer**?

(Most standard A-level courses at school or college would meet these requirements.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Blake is doing A-levels full-time at sixth form college. It's not funded by an employer or anything like that - just regular college.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Full-time A-levels at sixth form college is approved education.

**Next question about Blake:**

Is Blake working **less than 24 hours per week** (or not in paid work at all)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Blake doesn't work - just focuses on college.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question about Blake:**

Is Blake **NOT receiving any benefits in their own right**, such as Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit?

(In other words, Blake doesn't claim these benefits themselves?)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right, Blake doesn't get any benefits in their own name.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question about Blake:**

Is Blake doing an apprenticeship, or are they just doing regular A-levels at college?

(Since you mentioned Blake is studying A-levels at sixth form college, I'm assuming they're not in an apprenticeship - is that correct?)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's correct - Blake is just doing regular A-levels, not an apprenticeship.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: You've already confirmed you live in the UK (Manchester), so you meet the residency requirement. Let me continue to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Now for the responsibility check for Blake:**

Are you responsible for Blake? This means **ANY ONE** of the following:

1. **You live with Blake**
2. **You contribute to Blake's upkeep** (food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents – at least equal to the Child Benefit amount)
3. **You pay maintenance** for Blake under a court order or agreement

Does any of these apply to your situation with Blake?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Blake lives at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, Blake lives with you so you meet the responsibility requirement.

**Next question about Blake:**

Is there anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Blake, or planning to claim?

- **No** – no one else is claiming for Blake
- **Yes, but they've agreed I should claim instead** – there's another claimant but you've agreed between yourselves
- **Yes, and there's a dispute** – there's disagreement about who should claim

Which applies?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Blake's other parent lives with us too and they're already claiming Child Benefit for Blake.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Ah, I see. So Blake's other parent (who also lives with you) is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake.

**Important clarification:**

Only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child at a time. Since Blake's other parent is already receiving Child Benefit for Blake, you cannot also claim for the same child.

However, there are a couple of options:

1. **If you both agree** that you should be the one to claim instead, the current claimant can stop their claim and you can start a new one.

2. **If there's a disagreement** about who should claim, HMRC will need to decide who has priority (usually the person the child lives with, but since you all live together, it may need further review).

Are you and Blake's other parent in agreement about who should claim, or is this something you're looking to change/dispute?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, we're not looking to change anything - they're already claiming it and that's fine.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I understand - that's perfectly fine. Since Blake's other parent is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake and you're happy with that arrangement, there's no need for you to make a separate claim for Blake.

---

**Let me summarise the outcome of your assessment:**

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Summary of Your Child Benefit Eligibility

| Child | Age | Eligible for YOU to claim? | Reason |
|-------|-----|---------------------------|--------|
| **Alex** | 14 | ❌ No | You are not responsible for Alex (they live with your ex-partner and you don't contribute financially) |
| **Blake** | 19 | ❌ No (already claimed) | Blake's other parent is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake |

---

### Key Points:

**For Alex:**
- Although Alex is under 16 and would otherwise qualify, you cannot claim because you need to be "responsible" for the child – meaning you either live with them or contribute financially to their care.
- Your ex-partner (who Alex lives with) may be eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex if they aren't already.

**For Blake:**
- Blake meets all the eligibility criteria (in approved full-time education, under 20, not working full-time, no disqualifying benefits or apprenticeship).
- However, since Blake's other parent is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake, and you're happy with that arrangement, there's nothing further you need to do. The household is already receiving the benefit.

---

If your circumstances change in the future (e.g., if you start contributing to Alex's care or if Blake's other parent wants to transfer the claim to you), you may be able to revisit your eligibility. 

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

reasonings: Alex is 14 years old (under 16) and meets the age requirement. However, Alex lives with your ex-partner and you are not currently contributing financially to Alex's care. To claim Child Benefit, you must be 'responsible' for the child - which means either living with them OR contributing at least the Child Benefit amount towards their upkeep. Since neither applies, you are not eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex. Note: Your ex-partner (who Alex lives with) may be eligible to claim instead.

Blake is 19 years old and in approved full-time education (A-levels at sixth form college), meets all the child-related criteria (not working 24+ hours, not receiving benefits in their own name, not in a disqualifying apprenticeship), you meet the residency requirement (living in the UK), and you are responsible for Blake (they live with you). However, Blake's other parent is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake. Only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child at a time, and since you're happy with the current arrangement, there is no action needed from you.
arguments.overall_reasoning: You are not eligible to claim Child Benefit for either child in your current circumstances. For Alex (14), you don't meet the responsibility requirement as Alex lives with your ex-partner and you don't contribute financially. For Blake (19), while you would otherwise meet all eligibility requirements, Blake's other parent is already claiming Child Benefit and you've confirmed you're happy with that arrangement. Child Benefit can only be claimed by one person per child.

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 14 years old (under 16) and meets the age requirement. However, Alex lives with your ex-partner and you are not currently contributing financially to Alex's care. To claim Child Benefit, you must be 'responsible' for the child - which means either living with them OR contributing at least the Child Benefit amount towards their upkeep. Since neither applies, you are not eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex. Note: Your ex-partner (who Alex lives with) may be eligible to claim instead.

Blake is 19 years old and in approved full-time education (A-levels at sixth form college), meets all the child-related criteria (not working 24+ hours, not receiving benefits in their own name, not in a disqualifying apprenticeship), you meet the residency requirement (living in the UK), and you are responsible for Blake (they live with you). However, Blake's other parent is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake. Only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child at a time, and since you're happy with the current arrangement, there is no action needed from you.

response.overall_reasoning: You are not eligible to claim Child Benefit for either child in your current circumstances. For Alex (14), you don't meet the responsibility requirement as Alex lives with your ex-partner and you don't contribute financially. For Blake (19), while you would otherwise meet all eligibility requirements, Blake's other parent is already claiming Child Benefit and you've confirmed you're happy with that arrangement. Child Benefit can only be claimed by one person per child.
